name: Islam
tagline: Submission to the one God whose final word came through Muhammad.
overview: Islam is the world's second-largest religion, founded in 7th-century Arabia through the prophethood of Muhammad. It holds that there is one God (Allah) whose final, uncreated, and verbatim revelation came through the Quran. The five pillars — declaration of faith, prayer, fasting, almsgiving, and pilgrimage — structure Muslim religious life. Islam shares with Christianity an affirmation of one personal creator God, moral law, prophetic revelation, and final judgment, but diverges sharply on the nature of Jesus, the doctrine of the Trinity, and the integrity of the biblical text.
core_claims: There is no god but Allah, and Muhammad is his final messenger.
The Quran is the direct, uncreated, verbatim word of God — inerrant and unsupersedable.
Jesus (Isa) was a prophet and Messiah but not divine; he was not crucified.
The Christian and Jewish scriptures have been corrupted (tahrif) and superseded.
Salvation is by submission to God's will, good deeds, and his sovereign mercy.
christian_critique: Islam and Christianity share enough family resemblance — monotheism, prophethood, Scripture, judgment — that their differences are theologically precise rather than merely cultural. The central dispute is Christological: Islam affirms Jesus' miraculous birth and prophetic ministry but explicitly denies his crucifixion (Quran 4:157), resurrection, and divine nature. The crucifixion, however, is among the most historically secure facts of antiquity, confirmed by Tacitus, Josephus, and the Babylonian Talmud — none of whom had any motive to support Christian claims. The tahrif doctrine requires that thousands of manuscripts across multiple continents and centuries were systematically corrupted without a single trace in the textual record — a position no serious historian defends. The Trinitarian critique attacks tritheism, which Christianity itself condemned as heresy before Islam existed. The deeper question Islam cannot answer is soteriological: if Jesus was only a prophet, how are sins forgiven? Islam offers no atoning mediator — only deeds, divine mercy, and uncertainty.

name: Atheism & Naturalism
tagline: The physical universe is all that exists. There is no God, no soul, no transcendent meaning.
overview: Atheism in its contemporary form is almost always paired with philosophical naturalism — the view that the natural world, accessible to science, exhausts reality. There is no supernatural realm, no transcendent mind, no life after death. Consciousness, morality, and the sense of meaning are emergent properties of blind physical processes. Major contemporary proponents include Richard Dawkins, Daniel Dennett, Sam Harris, and in philosophy, Alex Rosenberg and Quentin Smith.
core_claims: The physical universe is all that exists — there is no supernatural realm.
Science is the only reliable path to knowledge about reality.
Consciousness and moral experience are products of evolution, not transcendent realities.
The burden of proof lies entirely with those who assert God's existence.
Human beings can live meaningful, ethical lives without religious belief.
key_texts: Alex Rosenberg, The Atheist's Guide to Reality (2011)
christian_critique: Naturalism faces what Thomas Nagel — himself an atheist — calls a structural explanatory failure: consciousness, the fine-tuning of the cosmos, the origin of the universe, and the objectivity of moral obligations all resist naturalistic explanation. Plantinga's Evolutionary Argument Against Naturalism (EAAN) demonstrates that if naturalism and evolution are both true, our cognitive faculties were selected for survival rather than truth — which defeats the very reasoning used to argue for naturalism. The moral argument cuts equally deep: naturalism may explain why we behave as if morality is real, but provides no account of why any of it is genuinely binding. Borrowed moral vocabulary — human dignity, universal rights, the evil of oppression — carries the freight of a Christian metaphysical framework that naturalism has abandoned. Christianity's invitation is not a retreat from reason but a demonstration that reason itself — its reliability, its purchase on truth — is only coherent within a theistic framework.

name: Mormonism
tagline: A restoration of lost Christianity through new prophetic revelation — with a fundamentally different God.
overview: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints was founded in 1830 by Joseph Smith, who claimed to receive golden plates containing the Book of Mormon. Mormonism presents itself as a restoration of true Christianity, lost through centuries of apostasy. It affirms the name of Jesus Christ and much Christian vocabulary, but the underlying theology is radically different: God the Father has a physical body, humans can become gods, and salvation is achieved through a combination of grace, ordinances, and exaltation through eternal progression.
core_claims: God the Father (Heavenly Father) has a physical, glorified body of flesh and bones.
The Father, Son, and Holy Ghost are three separate beings — not one God.
Joseph Smith was a prophet who restored the true church after a universal apostasy.
The Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, and Pearl of Great Price are scripture alongside the Bible.
Humans can progress to become gods (exaltation) — 'As man is, God once was; as God is, man may become.'
key_texts: Lorenzo Snow, The Teachings of Lorenzo Snow
christian_critique: Mormonism is not a variant of Christianity but a different religion sharing Christian vocabulary. The God of Mormonism is not the God of classical theism or the Westminster Confession: he is a finite, embodied, progressing deity who was once a man — a concept utterly foreign to the biblical witness ('God is not man,' Numbers 23:19; 'Before me no god was formed,' Isaiah 43:10). The claim of universal apostasy contradicts Christ's promise that the gates of hell would not prevail against His church (Matthew 16:18). The Book of Mormon has no archaeological, linguistic, or genetic corroboration for the civilizations it describes. The doctrine of exaltation — humans becoming gods — is precisely the temptation the serpent offered in Genesis 3:5. Mormonism's Jesus atoned for sins in Gethsemane rather than primarily on the cross, does not possess the eternal divine nature of the Logos of John 1, and is the spirit-brother of Lucifer. These are not peripheral differences but fundamental incompatibilities with historic Christian faith.

name: Jehovah's Witnesses
tagline: Only Jehovah is God. Jesus is a created being. The Trinity is a pagan invention.
overview: Jehovah's Witnesses originated in the 1870s through Charles Taze Russell. They reject the Trinity, holding that Jehovah alone is God and that Jesus Christ is his first and greatest creation. They use their own translation (the New World Translation) which systematically alters key Trinitarian texts. They deny the immortality of the soul, the existence of hell, and the bodily resurrection, believing instead in a future earthly paradise for faithful members.
core_claims: Jehovah alone is the Almighty God — Jesus is a created being, not co-equal with the Father.
The Holy Spirit is not a person but an impersonal active force.
The Trinity doctrine is a pagan invention introduced into Christianity by apostates.
Jesus rose spiritually, not bodily — his physical body was dissolved or preserved elsewhere.
Only 144,000 go to heaven; faithful members inherit a restored earthly paradise.
key_texts: New World Translation of the Holy Scriptures
christian_critique: Jehovah's Witnesses represent a modern form of Arianism — the 4th-century heresy Athanasius defeated at Nicaea. Their New World Translation renders John 1:1 as 'the Word was a god' — a translation rejected by virtually every Greek scholar because it violates standard rules of Greek grammar. Thomas's confession in John 20:28 ('My Lord and my God') and Colossians 2:9 ('in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily') present an insurmountable obstacle to their Christology. The Watch Tower Society has made multiple failed end-times predictions (1914, 1925, 1975) — each subsequently reinterpreted — which by the biblical standard of Deuteronomy 18:21-22 disqualifies them as prophetic authority. Their Christ cannot save because a created being cannot bear infinite divine penalty — only the eternally divine Son can.

name: Hinduism
tagline: Ultimate reality is one divine consciousness. The individual self is identical with Brahman.
overview: Hinduism is the world's oldest major religion. At its philosophical core (Advaita Vedanta) stands the claim that ultimate reality is Brahman — a single, undifferentiated, impersonal divine consciousness — and that the individual self (Atman) is ultimately identical with Brahman. The apparent diversity of the world is maya (illusion). Liberation (moksha) is the recognition of this identity. Popular Hinduism also includes devotional theism, polytheism, and elaborate ritual traditions.
core_claims: Brahman is the one ultimate reality — infinite, undifferentiated divine consciousness.
The individual self (Atman) is ultimately identical with Brahman.
The phenomenal world is maya — an illusion overlaid on true reality.
Karma and reincarnation govern the soul's journey across countless lifetimes.
Moksha — liberation from the cycle of rebirth — is the highest human goal.
christian_critique: Hinduism correctly identifies the inadequacy of purely materialist accounts of reality and affirms the primacy of consciousness. But an impersonal Brahman cannot love, covenant, act in history, or forgive — which means Hinduism has a sophisticated metaphysics but no soteriology adequate to the problem of moral guilt. The karma system is works-righteousness taken to a cosmic extreme: there is no grace, no forgiveness, only the inexorable accumulation and discharge of karmic debt across infinite lifetimes. The doctrine of maya undermines the significance of the Incarnation and Resurrection, which Christianity insists are real, datable, publicly witnessed acts of God in actual history. The self that Hinduism says must be dissolved into Brahman is the very self that Christianity says God created, loves, names, redeems, and glorifies.

name: Buddhism
tagline: Suffering arises from craving. Liberation comes through extinguishing the self.
overview: Buddhism originated in 5th-century BC India. It holds that all things are impermanent (anicca), marked by suffering (dukkha), and without a permanent self (anatta). Liberation (nirvana) is the cessation of craving and the dissolution of the illusion of selfhood. Buddhism does not affirm a creator God — reality operates through dependent origination. It encompasses major traditions including Theravada, Mahayana, and Vajrayana.
core_claims: All existence is characterized by impermanence, suffering, and the absence of a permanent self.
Suffering arises from craving and attachment to what is ultimately impermanent.
There is no creator God — the universe operates through dependent origination.
Liberation (nirvana) is achieved through the Noble Eightfold Path.
The self is an illusion — no permanent soul underlies conscious experience.
key_texts: Thich Nhat Hanh, The Heart of the Buddha's Teaching
christian_critique: Christianity and Buddhism share a diagnostic agreement: human existence as ordinarily lived is disordered. But they diverge on cause, cure, and goal. Buddhism locates the problem in desire; Christianity in sin — a moral rupture with a personal God that no meditative discipline can repair. Buddhism's cure is extinguishing craving; Christianity's is atonement. Buddhism's goal is dissolution of the self; Christianity's is the glorification and resurrection of the personal self in eternal communion with God. The anatta doctrine faces internal coherence problems: if the self is an illusion, it is unclear who practices the Eightfold Path or achieves liberation. Buddhism offers therapeutic wisdom but not forgiveness.

name: Secular Humanism
tagline: Human reason and compassion are sufficient. No God is needed for a good life.
overview: Secular humanism affirms the dignity and worth of human beings without reference to the supernatural. It holds that human reason, ethics, and philosophical inquiry are sufficient guides to the good life. It champions human autonomy, scientific inquiry, and democratic values. It functions as the dominant implicit worldview of Western cultural institutions — universities, media, government — often as an unexamined default rather than a consciously chosen philosophy.
core_claims: Human beings are the measure of value and the source of moral purpose.
Reason and science are the appropriate and sufficient tools for understanding reality.
Ethics are derived from human experience and flourishing — not divine command.
Life has meaning and purpose without reference to God or an afterlife.
Human problems are best addressed through rational, cooperative, democratic effort.
key_texts: Paul Kurtz, Forbidden Fruit: The Ethics of Secularism
Bertrand Russell, Why I Am Not a Christian
christian_critique: Secular humanism is the worldview most guilty of philosophical parasitism. It inherited the moral vocabulary of Christian civilization — human dignity, universal rights, the intrinsic worth of persons — without the metaphysical foundations that gave those concepts force. Nietzsche recognized this and called it 'the shadow of God.' The central question secular humanism cannot answer: on what basis is a human being intrinsically valuable if there is no God? Evolution produces organisms, not persons with inalienable worth. The 20th century demonstrated what consistently secular regimes produce when they abandon transcendent grounding: the most systematic atrocities in recorded history were committed by states that explicitly rejected the Christian framework.

name: Judaism
tagline: The covenant people of God await a Messiah yet to come. Jesus was not him.
overview: Judaism is the oldest Abrahamic faith and the root from which Christianity emerged. It affirms the one God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob who revealed himself through Torah and the prophets. Rabbinic Judaism centers on Torah study, prayer, Sabbath observance, and synagogue community. It rejects New Testament claims about Jesus, holding that he did not fulfill messianic expectations and that the Torah remains the fullness of divine revelation.
core_claims: The God of Israel — one, indivisible, incorporeal — revealed himself through Torah and the prophets.
The Jewish people are God's covenant people, chosen to be a light to the nations.
Jesus of Nazareth was not the Messiah — the messianic age he was expected to inaugurate did not arrive.
The Torah is the complete and binding revelation of God's will for human life.
The Messiah will be a human king who rebuilds the Temple, gathers the exiles, and brings world peace.
christian_critique: Christianity does not claim to replace Judaism with a foreign religion but to fulfill it. Jesus is the Messiah the Hebrew prophets foretold: born in Bethlehem (Micah 5:2), entering Jerusalem on a donkey (Zechariah 9:9), betrayed for 30 pieces of silver (Zechariah 11:12), his hands and feet pierced (Psalm 22:16), bearing the iniquity of 'us all' (Isaiah 53). The Dead Sea Scrolls — predating Christ by 200-300 years — contain Isaiah 53 substantially identical to the text used today, eliminating any claim of post-Christian interpolation. The destruction of the Temple in 70 AD makes complete sense within Christianity — the ultimate sacrifice had already been offered. The most honest engagement with Judaism acknowledges its deep truth — the holiness of God, the reality of covenant, the certainty of judgment — while presenting Jesus as the one in whom every promise finds its yes and amen (2 Corinthians 1:20).
